items.prompt: A function only needs to determine whether any value appears more than once. Which state is sufficient during a left-to-right scan?

options.text: A set of values seen so far.
A full frequency map is always required.
Only the immediately previous value.
An output array containing every processed value.

feedback.reason: Presence state is sufficient when the result depends only on whether a value appeared before.
feedback.details: The scan checks whether the current value is already in the seen set. If it is, a duplicate exists. Exact counts are unnecessary because the contract asks only for a yes-or-no answer.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.frequency_map_required: Counts would work, but they retain more information than the contract requires.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.previous_value_only: A repeated value may be separated from its earlier occurrence in an unsorted input.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.output_array: Storing the processed prefix does not provide efficient membership state and retains unnecessary data.

items.prompt: A report must return the exact number of occurrences of every distinct value. Which state matches the contract?

options.text: A mapping from each value to its current count.
A set recording whether each value appeared.
One boolean indicating whether any duplicate exists.
One variable containing the previous input value.

feedback.reason: Exact multiplicity requires a count for each distinct value.
feedback.details: A frequency map distinguishes one occurrence from two, three, or more. Presence-only state collapses all positive counts into the same fact and therefore cannot reproduce exact frequencies.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.seen_set: A set records presence but loses the number of occurrences.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicate_flag: One boolean cannot identify which values occurred or how often.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.previous_value: The previous value provides only local adjacency information, not global counts.

items.prompt: A function must return the index of the first occurrence of every distinct value. Which mapping should it maintain?

options.text: Map each value to its index only when that value is first encountered.
Overwrite the stored index every time the value appears.
Map each value only to its total count.
Map each index to the value already stored at that index.

feedback.reason: The state update rule must preserve the first index rather than replace it.
feedback.details: When a value is absent from the map, its current index becomes the first occurrence. Later occurrences must not overwrite that entry. A count alone does not preserve position.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.value_to_latest_index: Overwriting implements a last-occurrence contract, not a first-occurrence contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.value_to_count: Counts do not identify where the first occurrence appeared.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.index_to_value: The input already provides index-to-value access; this mapping does not answer first-index queries efficiently.

items.prompt: A function must return the index of the last occurrence of every value. Which update rule is correct during a left-to-right scan?

options.text: Assign the current index to the value on every occurrence.
Store the index only when the value is first encountered.
Increment the stored index by one on every occurrence.
Store only the number of occurrences.

feedback.reason: For a last-occurrence contract, each newer position must replace the older one.
feedback.details: After the full scan, the most recent assignment for each value is its final position. Write-once state would preserve the first occurrence instead.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.write_only_when_absent: This retains the first index and ignores later occurrences.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.increment_index: The next integer after an old index is not necessarily the position of the new occurrence.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.store_count_only: A count does not identify the final position.

items.prompt: The input consists only of lowercase English letters, and the algorithm needs exact character frequencies. Which state is the most direct?

options.text: An array of 26 counters indexed by character.
A copy of every character processed so far.
One counter shared by all letters.
Only the previous character.

feedback.reason: A small fixed domain supports direct indexed frequency state.
feedback.details: Each lowercase letter maps to one stable counter. The array size is independent of input length, so it is both simple and sufficient for exact frequencies.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.store_entire_prefix: The full prefix contains the needed information but retains far more state than necessary.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.single_counter: One counter cannot distinguish frequencies of different letters.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.previous_character: Local adjacency state cannot recover global character counts.

items.prompt: The input contains arbitrary 64-bit integers, and only values that actually occur should consume state. Which representation is appropriate for exact counts?

options.text: A hash map from encountered value to count.
An array indexed directly by every possible 64-bit integer.
One boolean shared by all values.
A counter that resets whenever the current value differs from the previous one.

feedback.reason: A sparse, very large value domain calls for state allocated only for encountered keys.
feedback.details: A hash map stores one entry per distinct observed value. Direct indexing over the full 64-bit domain is infeasible, while adjacent-run counting does not capture separated occurrences.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.array_by_numeric_value: The theoretical index range is far too large to allocate directly.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.seen_boolean: One boolean cannot distinguish values or preserve exact counts.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.adjacent_count: Separated occurrences of the same value would be counted as different runs.

items.prompt: Arrange the steps for detecting a repeated value while scanning an unsorted sequence with a seen set.

elements.text: Check whether the value is already in the seen set.
Report repetition if present; otherwise insert the value.

feedback.reason: Membership must be checked against prior state before the current occurrence is inserted.
feedback.details: The seen set represents values from earlier positions. Inserting first would make every current value appear previously seen and would destroy the distinction between first and repeated occurrences.

items.prompt: When is presence-only state sufficient? Select all that apply.

options.text: The decision depends only on whether the value appeared before.
The output needs the set of distinct values but not their counts.
The output must preserve the exact number of occurrences.
The output must return the first index of each value.

feedback.reason: Presence state is sufficient only when counts and positional metadata are irrelevant.
feedback.details: A set preserves membership. It can answer whether a value appeared and can represent the distinct values. It cannot recover exact multiplicity or occurrence positions.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.need_exact_multiplicity: Presence collapses every positive count into one state and loses exact multiplicity.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.need_first_index: A set does not store the position of the first occurrence.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.need_any_prior_occurrence: Prior membership is exactly the fact represented by a seen set.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.need_distinct_membership: A set directly represents which distinct values occur.

items.prompt: A left-to-right scan processes n values and stores one hash-map entry per distinct value. Select the expected time and auxiliary-space complexity.
constraints: Hash lookup and update are expected O(1) per processed value.
d is the number of distinct input values.

feedback.reason: The algorithm performs one expected-constant update per input occurrence and retains one entry per distinct key.
feedback.details: Processing n values gives expected O(n) time. The map size follows d, not the number of loop statements. In the worst case d equals n, but O(d) states the tighter relationship.

items.prompt: A function must return all values that occur exactly once. Which state is necessary before producing the result?

options.text: A frequency count for each distinct value.
Only a set of values that have appeared at least once.
One boolean indicating whether any duplicate exists.
Only the immediately previous value.

feedback.reason: Selecting values with count exactly one requires distinguishing one occurrence from two or more.
feedback.details: A presence set cannot tell whether a value appeared once or repeatedly. Exact counts provide the post-scan predicate count[value] equals one.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.seen_once_set: A normal seen set retains only whether a value ever appeared, not how many times.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicate_exists_flag: A global flag cannot identify which particular values are unique.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.previous_value_only: Separated occurrences would not be combined into one global count.

items.prompt: When is comparing only the current value with the previous value sufficient for duplicate-related reasoning?

options.text: When the contract concerns consecutive runs, or equal values are guaranteed to be grouped.
For detecting all repeated values in any unsorted input.
For computing exact global frequencies of arbitrary values.
For returning the first index of every distinct value.

feedback.reason: Previous-value state represents local adjacency, not global history.
feedback.details: It is sufficient when only consecutive equality matters or when preprocessing guarantees that equal values form one run. Otherwise, separated occurrences require broader seen or frequency state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.any_unsorted_duplicates: A repeated value can reappear after many unrelated values.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.exact_global_counts: Local run lengths do not combine separated occurrences without a grouping guarantee.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_index_lookup: The previous value does not preserve the first index of every distinct key.

items.prompt: Which statements correctly distinguish presence state from frequency state? Select all that apply.

options.text: A set can answer whether a value has appeared.
A frequency map can distinguish one occurrence from several.
Either structure may grow with the number of distinct values.
A set directly preserves the exact count of every value.

feedback.reason: Presence and multiplicity are different information contracts, while both structures may scale with distinct keys.
feedback.details: A set stores membership. A frequency map stores membership plus a count. Choosing between them should follow the information required by the result, not an assumption that one is always constant-space.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.set_preserves_exact_counts: A standard set records only whether a key is present.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.set_answers_membership: Membership is the core fact represented by a set.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.map_preserves_multiplicity: The numeric value associated with each key preserves exact occurrence count.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.both_may_grow_with_keys: Both may contain one entry per distinct observed value.

items.prompt: Which statements describe a correct exact-frequency invariant during a left-to-right scan? Select all that apply.

options.text: After processing a prefix, count[x] equals the number of occurrences of x in that prefix.
Before a value is first seen, its logical count is zero.
Each processed occurrence increments exactly one counter once.
The count of the previous value must reset whenever the current value changes.

feedback.reason: Exact frequencies are maintained as cumulative counts over the processed prefix.
feedback.details: A missing key represents zero earlier occurrences, and every new occurrence increases its own value's count once. Counts do not reset merely because another value appears.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.reset_on_change: Resetting on value changes would count adjacent runs rather than global frequency.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.processed_prefix_count: This is the central correctness invariant for the frequency table.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.missing_means_zero: Zero is the correct prior count before the first occurrence.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.increment_once: One occurrence contributes exactly one to its value's multiplicity.

items.prompt: An algorithm uses one loop but stores every distinct value encountered. Which conclusion is correct?

options.text: The running time may be linear, but auxiliary space can still grow with the distinct-value count.
One loop guarantees O(1) auxiliary space.
Any growing state makes the running time O(n²).
All stored seen-state is automatically output space.

feedback.reason: Iteration count and retained memory are independent complexity dimensions.
feedback.details: A single pass can perform O(n) expected work while keeping O(d) auxiliary state. The seen structure is auxiliary unless it is itself the required returned result.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_loop_constant_space: Loop count does not bound the size of a collection created inside the loop.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.growing_state_quadratic_time: State growth does not imply that each operation scans all stored entries.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.state_is_output: Working memory and returned output must be classified according to their roles, not merely their contents.

items.prompt: What information is lost when a frequency map is replaced by a set? Select all that apply.

options.text: The exact count of each value.
The ability to distinguish one occurrence from multiple occurrences.
Whether a value appears at all.
Which distinct values appear.

feedback.reason: A set preserves keys but discards multiplicity.
feedback.details: Membership and the distinct-key collection remain available. What disappears is the numeric information needed to distinguish one, two, or more occurrences.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.membership: Membership is exactly what a set preserves.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.distinct_keys: The elements of the set are the distinct keys.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.exact_count: A set has no field that records the numeric frequency associated with a key.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.single_vs_multiple: Both one occurrence and many occurrences reduce to the same present state in a set.

items.prompt: An algorithm counts characters using an array of 26 integers while scanning a string of length n. Select the time and auxiliary-space complexity under the fixed lowercase-English-alphabet model.
constraints: The alphabet size is fixed at 26.
The result does not return the counter array.

feedback.reason: The scan grows with n, while the counter array has a fixed size independent of n.
feedback.details: Processing each character gives O(n) time. A 26-entry array is O(26), which is also O(1) asymptotically because the alphabet is fixed. Both accepted forms describe the same fixed-domain memory bound.

items.prompt: A task only asks whether every value occurs an even number of times. Which state can be sufficient without storing full counts?

options.text: One parity bit or boolean per distinct value, toggled on each occurrence.
A complete copy of the input sequence.
Only the immediately previous value.
One global parity bit shared by all values.

feedback.reason: The state should retain exactly the information required by the final predicate.
feedback.details: For an even-versus-odd decision, exact counts are unnecessary. Toggling one parity state per value preserves whether that value has appeared an odd or even number of times.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.full_input_copy: The full sequence retains far more information than the parity contract requires.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.previous_value: Separated occurrences of the same value would not be combined.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.global_parity: One global bit cannot distinguish the parity of different values.

items.prompt: Two strings contain lowercase English letters. The task is to determine whether they contain the same characters with the same multiplicities, while character order is irrelevant. Which approach matches the required state?

options.text: Build character-frequency arrays and compare all counters.
Compare only the sets of characters that appear.
Compare only the first character of each string.
Equal lengths alone prove equal multiplicities.

feedback.reason: Equal multiplicities require count state for every relevant character.
feedback.details: Because order is irrelevant, positions need not be preserved. However, presence alone is insufficient: strings can use the same distinct characters with different counts. Frequency arrays preserve exactly the needed information.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.compare_seen_sets: Sets cannot distinguish different multiplicities of the same characters.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.compare_first_characters: The first position does not summarize the remaining characters or their counts.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.compare_lengths_only: Equal total length does not imply that each character count matches.

items.prompt: An algorithm scans n values from a domain of size R, updates an R-entry counter array, and then scans all R counters to find the most frequent value. Select its time, auxiliary-space, and output-space complexity.
constraints: The result is one value and one count.

feedback.reason: The algorithm traverses both the input and the counter domain, while the counters are temporary state.
feedback.details: Updating counts costs O(n), and finding the maximum across R counters costs O(R). The R counters are auxiliary space; returning one value and count is constant-size output.

items.prompt: A function scans n values, builds a temporary frequency map with d keys, and returns a new array containing the k values whose frequency is exactly one. Select its expected time, auxiliary-space, and output-space complexity.

feedback.reason: The map stores one count per distinct key, while the returned array stores only qualifying values.
feedback.details: The input scan is expected O(n), and iterating over d map entries is O(d), which is bounded by n. The temporary map is O(d) auxiliary space, and the required result is O(k) output space.
